statement.markdown:
Write a function to validate the strict nesting of HTML tags in a given string. The function should return `true` if the nesting is valid and `false` otherwise. A nesting is considered valid if every opening tag has a matching closing tag, and every closing tag matches the corresponding opening tag. For example, `

Hello, World!

` is a valid nesting, but `

Hello, World!

` is not.
statement.inputFormat: The input string is a sequence of HTML tags, where each tag is represented as a string of the form `<tag_name>...</tag_name>` or `</tag_name>`. The input string may contain multiple levels of nesting.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return a boolean value indicating whether the nesting is valid.
statement.notes: You can assume that the input string only contains valid HTML tag names. The function should be efficient and handle large input strings.

constraints.description: The maximum number of HTML tags in the input string

examples.explanation: The nesting is valid because every opening tag has a matching closing tag.

The nesting is invalid because the closing tag `

` does not match the opening tag `

` in the corresponding opening tag ``.

hints.text: Use a stack data structure to keep track of the opening tags.

editorial.title: Editorial: Validating Strict Nested HTML Structure
editorial.intuition: The key insight is to use a stack to keep track of the opening tags. When we encounter a closing tag, we pop the corresponding opening tag from the stack and check if the tags match.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach would involve parsing the input string and checking each pair of opening and closing tags.
editorial.betterSolution: We can improve the brute-force approach by using a stack to store the opening tags.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a stack to keep track of the opening tags and checking each closing tag against the top of the stack.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The solution is correct because it ensures that every opening tag has a matching closing tag and that every closing tag matches the corresponding opening tag.
commonMistakes: Failing to check if the stack is empty when popping an opening tag
